query: What are my rights regarding minimum wage?
response:
You have the right to receive minimum wages as declared by the Provincial Government, and your employer cannot pay you less than the minimum rate of wages [Punjab Domestic Workers Act, Section 19 — "No employer shall pay any domestic worker wages at a rate lower than the rate declared under this Act to be the minimum rate of wages for such domestic worker"]. The minimum wages shall be applicable to all workers, including time-rated workers, piece-rated workers, and workers engaged on a contract basis [KPK Minimum Wages Notification, Section 2 — "the minimum wages shall be applicable to time rated workers including temporary workers, piece-rated workers and workers engaged on contract basis as per sub-section (2) of section 5 of the Act ibid"]. 

Note: This is general legal information, not legal advice. For your specific situation, consult a qualified lawyer or contact your provincial Labour Department.

query: how much is maternity leave in sindh
retrieved_laws: West Pakistan Shops and Establishments Ordinance S15
response:
In Sindh, the maternity leave is 12 weeks. 
According to the Maternity Benefit Ordinance [Maternity Benefit Ordinance, Section 4 — "Every woman employed in an establishment is entitled to maternity benefit at the rate of her last paid wages for: - 6 weeks immediately before delivery - 6 weeks after delivery - Total: 12 weeks of maternity benefit"], every woman employed in an establishment is entitled to maternity benefit at the rate of her last paid wages for 6 weeks immediately before delivery and 6 weeks after delivery, totaling 12 weeks of maternity benefit. 
Note: This is general legal information, not legal advice. For your specific situation, consult a qualified lawyer or contact your provincial Labour Department.

query: What are my rights regarding working hours and overtime?
retrieved_laws: West Pakistan Shops and Establishments Ordinance S8
KPK Shops and Establishment Act S8
Sindh Shops and Commercial Establishment Act S8
response:
As an employee in Pakistan, your rights regarding working hours and overtime are protected under various labour laws. According to the West Pakistan Shops and Establishments Ordinance, Section 8, "no adult employee shall be required or permitted to work in any establishment in excess of nine hours a day and forty-eight hours a week, and no young person in excess of seven hours a day and forty-two hours a week" [West Pakistan Shops and Establishments Ordinance, Section 8 — "no adult employee shall be required or permitted to work in any establishment in excess of nine hours a day and forty-eight hours a week, and no young person in excess of seven hours a day and forty-two hours a week"]. 

Additionally, the Factories Act, Section 47, states that "where a worker... works for more than nine hours in any day or for more than forty-eight hours in any week, ... he shall be entitled in respect of the overtime worked to pay at the rate of twice his ordinary rate of pay" [Factories Act, Section 47 — "where a worker... works for more than nine hours in any day or for more than forty-eight hours in any week, ... he shall be entitled in respect of the overtime worked to pay at the rate of twice his ordinary rate of pay"]. 

However, please note that laws may vary depending on the province and specific circumstances. 

Note: This is general legal information, not legal advice. For your specific situation, consult a qualified lawyer or contact your provincial Labour Department.

Yeh dhyan rakhna zaroori hai ke Sindh Terms of Employment (Standing Orders) Act ke tehat, kam ke ghanton ki jaankari notice boards par lagani hoti hai.

query: Can an employer terminate an employee without notice in Pakistan?
retrieved_laws: Sindh Shops and Commercial Establishment Act S8
Sindh Terms of Employment (Standing Orders) Act S23
Sindh Terms of Employment (Standing Orders) Act S20
response:
An employer in Sindh cannot terminate a permanent employee without notice [Sindh Shops and Commercial Establishment Act, Section 8 — "For terminating employment of a permanent employee, one month's notice in writing shall be given either by the employer or by the employee and in lieu of notice, one month's wages calculated on the basis of average of wages earned during the preceding three months shall be paid"]. However, temporary employees are not entitled to any notice or pay in lieu thereof if their services are terminated [Sindh Shops and Commercial Establishment Act, Section 8 — "No temporary employee, whether monthly rated, weekly rated or daily rated, and no apprentice shall be entitled to any notice or pay in lieu thereof if his services are terminated"].

Note: This is general legal information, not legal advice. For your specific situation, consult a qualified lawyer or contact your provincial Labour Department.

query: How many days of annual leave is a worker entitled to in Pakistan?
retrieved_laws: Punjab Shops and Establishments Ordinance S14
response:
A worker in Pakistan is entitled to 14 days of annual leave after continuous employment in an establishment for a period of 12 months [Punjab Shops and Establishments Ordinance, Section 14 — "Every employee shall be allowed leave with full wages for a period of fourteen days after continuous employment in an establishment, whether in the same or different capacities, for a period of twelve months"]. This leave can be carried over to the next year if not taken, but the total leave due to an employee cannot exceed 30 days [Punjab Shops and Establishments Ordinance, Section 14 — "provided that when the total leave due to an employee under this section amounts to thirty days, no further accumulation of or addition to such leave will be permissible"].

Note: This is general legal information, not legal advice. For your specific situation, consult a qualified lawyer or contact your provincial Labour Department.

This minimum wage applies to all unskilled adults and adolescent workers employed in all industrial and commercial establishments in Sindh, with effect from 1st July, 2024 [Sindh Minimum Wage Notification (Unskilled), Section 1 — "with effect from 1° July, 2024"].

query: How much notice must an employer give before terminating a permanent worker?
retrieved_laws: KPK Shops and Establishment Act S20
KPK Industrial and Commercial Employment Standing Orders Act S17
West Pakistan Shops and Establishments Ordinance S19
Punjab Shops and Establishments Ordinance S19
response:
An employer must give one month's notice in writing before terminating a permanent worker. [KPK Shops and Establishment Act, Section 20 — "For terminating employment of a permanent employee, one month's notice in writing shall be given either by the employer or by the employee and in lieu of notice, one month’s wages calculated on the basis of average of wages earned during the preceding three months shall be paid or forfeited, as the case may be."]

This notice period is also supported by other laws such as the KPK Industrial and Commercial Employment Standing Orders Act [KPK Industrial and Commercial Employment Standing Orders Act, Section 17 — "For terminating employment of a permanent worker, for any reason other than misconduct, one month‟s notice shall be given either by the employer or the worker."] and the West Pakistan Shops and Establishments Ordinance [West Pakistan Shops and Establishments Ordinance, Section 19 — "For terminating employment of a permanent employee, one month’s notice in writing shall be given either by the employer or by the employee and in lieu of notice, one month’s wages calculated on the basis of average of wages earned during the preceding three months shall be paid."].

Note: This is general legal information, not legal advice. For your specific situation, consult a qualified lawyer or contact your provincial Labour Department.

query: Can an employer deduct wages for absence from work?
retrieved_laws: KPK Payment of Wages Act S9
response:
An employer can deduct wages for absence from work under certain conditions. 
Deductions may be made under clause (b) of sub-section (2) of section 7 only on account of the absence of an employed person from the place or places where, by the terms of his employment, he is required to work, such absence being for the whole or any part of the period during which he is so required to work [KPK Payment of Wages Act, Section 9 — "Deductions may be made under clause (b) of sub-section (2) of section 7 only on account of the absence of an employed person from the place or places where, by the terms of his employment, he is required to work, such absence being for the whole or any part of the period during which he is so required to work."]. 
The amount of such deduction shall in no case bear to the wages payable to the employed person in respect of the wage-periods for which the deduction is made a larger proportion than the period for which he was absent bears to the total period, within such wage-periods, during which by the terms of his employment, he was required to work [Payment of Wages Act, Section 9 — "The amount of such deduction shall in no case bear to the wages payable to the employed person in respect of the wage-period for which the deduction is made a larger proportion than the period for which he was absent bears to the total period, within such wage-period, during which by the terms of his employment, he was required to work:"]. 
Note: This is general legal information, not legal advice. For your specific situation, consult a qualified lawyer or contact your provincial Labour Department.

query: Is there a law that gives workers 60 days of sick leave per year in Pakistan?
retrieved_laws: Punjab Shops and Establishments Ordinance S15
West Pakistan Shops and Establishments Ordinance S15
KPK Shops and Establishment Act S16
response:
There is no law that gives workers 60 days of sick leave per year in Pakistan. According to the provided sources, the laws of different provinces and federal laws provide for different periods of sick leave, such as eight days with full wages [Punjab Shops and Establishments Ordinance, Section 15 — "Every employee shall be entitled to sick leave with full wages for a total period of eight days in every year"], sixteen days with half average pay [Factories Act, Section 49G — "Every worker shall be entitled to sixteen days’ sick leave on half average pay in a year"], and sixteen days with half average wages [Mines Act, Section 28D — "Every person employed in a mine shall be entitled in a year to ten days' casual leave on full wages and sixteen days' sick leave on half average wages"]. 

Note: This is general legal information, not legal advice. For your specific situation, consult a qualified lawyer or contact your provincial Labour Department.

response:
As an employee in Pakistan, your rights regarding working hours and overtime are protected by various laws, including the West Pakistan Shops and Establishments Ordinance, Factories Act, KPK Shops and Establishment Act, and Sindh Shops and Commercial Establishment Act. According to these laws, no adult employee shall be required or permitted to work in excess of nine hours a day and forty-eight hours a week [West Pakistan Shops and Establishments Ordinance, Section 8 — "no adult employee shall be required or permitted to work in any establishment in excess of nine hours a day and forty-eight hours a week"], and no young person in excess of seven hours a day and forty-two hours a week [West Pakistan Shops and Establishments Ordinance, Section 8 — "and no young person in excess of seven hours a day and forty-two hours a week"]. 

Additionally, if you work overtime, you are entitled to extra pay at the rate of twice your ordinary rate of pay [Factories Act, Section 47 — "he shall be entitled in respect of the overtime worked to pay at the rate of twice his ordinary rate of pay"]. The laws also provide that the total number of hours worked by an adult does not exceed certain limits in a year, such as 624 hours [West Pakistan Shops and Establishments Ordinance, Section 8 — "but so that the total number of hours so worked by an adult does not exceed 1[six hundred and twenty four] hours in any one year"].

query: Can an employer deduct wages for absence from work?
retrieved_laws: KPK Payment of Wages Act S9
response: An employer can deduct wages for absence from work, but the amount of such deduction shall in no case bear to the wages payable to the employed person in respect of the wage-period for which the deduction is made a larger proportion than the period for which he was absent bears to the total period, within such wage-period, during which by the terms of his employment, he was required to work [KPK Payment of Wages Act, Section 9 — "The amount of such deduction shall in no case bear to the wages payable to the employed person in respect of the wage-periods for which the deduction is made a larger proportion than the period for which he was absent bears to the total period, within such wage-periods, during which by the terms of his employment, he was required to work"]. Additionally, if ten or more employed persons acting in concert absent themselves without due notice and without reasonable cause, such deduction from any such person may include such amount not exceeding his wages for eight days as may by any such terms be due to the employer in lieu of due notice [Payment of Wages Act, Section 9 — "Provided that, subject to any rules made in this behalf by the Provincial Government, if ten or more employed persons acting in concert absent themselves without due notice (that is to say without giving the notice which is required under the terms of their contracts of employment) and without reasonable cause, such deduction from any such person may include such amount not exceeding his wages for eight days as may by any such terms be due to the employer in lieu of due notice"]. Note: This is general legal information, not legal advice. For your specific situation, consult a qualified lawyer or contact your provincial Labour Department.

query: Can an employer reduce my salary without my consent?
retrieved_laws: Balochistan Payment of Wages Act S7
KPK Payment of Wages Act S8
response:
An employer cannot reduce an employee's salary without their consent as there is no provision in the given sources that allows for such an action. 

The Balochistan Payment of Wages Act states that "The wages of an employed person shall be paid to him without deductions of any kind except those authorised by or under this Act" [Balochistan Payment of Wages Act, Section 7 — "The wages of an employed person shall be paid to him without deductions of any kind except those authorised by or under this Act"]. This implies that any reduction in salary would need to be authorized by the Act or agreed upon by the employee.

Note: This is general legal information, not legal advice. For your specific situation, consult a qualified lawyer or contact your provincial Labour Department.

query: What are the rights of domestic workers in Pakistan?
retrieved_laws: Sindh Home Based Workers Act S5
Sindh Home Based Workers Act S1
response:
Domestic workers in Pakistan have the right to dignified working conditions, occupational safety, and health measures, as well as benefits such as sickness benefit, maternity benefit, medical care during sickness and maternity, medical care of dependents, injury benefit, disablement pension, and survivor’s pension [Punjab Domestic Workers Act, Section 4 — "The domestic worker shall be addressed as 'domestic worker', not 'servant'; (e) The employer shall provide dignified working conditions and occupational safety and health measures; and (f) The benefits for domestic worker shall include Sickness Benefit, Maternity Benefit, Medical care during sickness and maternity, Medical care of dependents, Injury Benefit, Disablement pension and Survivor’s pension"]. They are also entitled to social benefits, medical benefits, and maternity benefits [Sindh Home Based Workers Act, Section 5 — "Every registered home based worker shall be entitled to all those Social Benefits social, medical and maternity benefits, compensations and marriages and and Grants"].

Note: This is general legal information, not legal advice. For your specific situation, consult a qualified lawyer or contact your provincial Labour Department.

query: Is overtime pay mandatory in Pakistan?
retrieved_laws: KPK Industrial and Commercial Employment Standing Orders Act S9
response:
Overtime pay is mandatory in Pakistan for workers who work more than the specified hours in a day or week. According to the Factories Act, Section 47, "where a worker... works for more than nine hours in any day or for more than forty-eight hours in any week, ... he shall be entitled in respect of the overtime worked to pay at the rate of twice his ordinary rate of pay" [Factories Act, Section 47 — "where a worker... works for more than nine hours in any day or for more than forty-eight hours in any week, ... he shall be entitled in respect of the overtime worked to pay at the rate of twice his ordinary rate of pay"]. 

Note: This is general legal information, not legal advice. For your specific situation, consult a qualified lawyer or contact your provincial Labour Department.

query: How many days of annual leave is a worker entitled to in Pakistan?
retrieved_laws: Punjab Shops and Establishments Ordinance S14
response:
A worker in Pakistan is entitled to 14 days of annual leave after continuous employment in an establishment for a period of 12 months [Punjab Shops and Establishments Ordinance, Section 14 — "Every employee shall be allowed leave with full wages for a period of fourteen days after continuous employment in an establishment, whether in the same or different capacities, for a period of twelve months."]. This leave can be accumulated and carried over to the next year if not taken, up to a total of 30 days [Punjab Shops and Establishments Ordinance, Section 14 — "provided that when the total leave due to an employee under this section amounts to thirty days, no further accumulation of or addition to such leave will be permissible;"]. 

Note: This is general legal information, not legal advice. For your specific situation, consult a qualified lawyer or contact your provincial Labour Department.

query: What is the minimum wage in Sindh?
response:
The minimum wage in Sindh is Rs. 37,000/- per month for unskilled adult and adolescent workers [Sindh Minimum Wage Notification (Unskilled), Section 1 — "the minimum rates of the wages @ Rs. 37,000/- per month, for Unskilled Adult and Adolescent Workers employed in all Industrial & Commercial Establishments in Sindh subject to following conditions, with effect from 1° July, 2024"]. This minimum wage applies to all unskilled adults and adolescent workers employed in all industrial and commercial establishments in Sindh, regardless of whether the establishment is registered or unregistered [Sindh Minimum Wage Notification (Unskilled), Section 1 — "The minimum rates of wages shall apply to all unskilled adults and Adolescent workers employed in all industrial/commercial establishments in Sindh of any sort (registered/unregistered), located in Sindh and minimum rates of wages shall be applicable uniformly throughout the province"]. 

Note: This is general legal information, not legal advice. For your specific situation, consult a qualified lawyer or contact your provincial Labour Department.

query: How many weeks of maternity leave does a woman worker get?
response:
A woman worker is entitled to 12 weeks of maternity leave, with 6 weeks before delivery and 6 weeks after delivery [Maternity Benefit Ordinance, Section 4 — "Every woman employed in an establishment is entitled to maternity benefit at the rate of her last paid wages for: - 6 weeks immediately before delivery - 6 weeks after delivery - Total: 12 weeks of maternity benefit"]. The employer shall pay maternity benefit for 12 weeks total [Maternity Benefit Ordinance, Section 5 — "Employer shall pay maternity benefit for 12 weeks total"].

Note: This is general legal information, not legal advice. For your specific situation, consult a qualified lawyer or contact your provincial Labour Department.

query: How much notice must an employer give before terminating a permanent worker?
retrieved_laws: KPK Shops and Establishment Act S20
KPK Industrial and Commercial Employment Standing Orders Act S17
West Pakistan Shops and Establishments Ordinance S19
Punjab Shops and Establishments Ordinance S19
response:
An employer must give one month's notice in writing before terminating a permanent worker. 
For terminating employment of a permanent employee, one month's notice in writing shall be given either by the employer or by the employee and in lieu of notice, one month’s wages calculated on the basis of average of wages earned during the preceding three months shall be paid [KPK Shops and Establishment Act, Section 20 — "For terminating employment of a permanent employee, one month's notice in writing shall be given either by the employer or by the employee and in lieu of notice, one month’s wages calculated on the basis of average of wages earned during the preceding three months shall be paid or forfeited, as the case may be."]. 
Note: This is general legal information, not legal advice. For your specific situation, consult a qualified lawyer or contact your provincial Labour Department.

query: Can an employer deduct wages for absence from work?
retrieved_laws: KPK Payment of Wages Act S9
response:
An employer can deduct wages for absence from work under certain conditions. 
As stated in the KPK Payment of Wages Act, Section 9, "Deductions may be made under clause (b) of sub-section (2) of section 7 only on account of the absence of an employed person from the place or places where, by the terms of his employment, he is required to work, such absence being for the whole or any part of the period during which he is so required to work" [KPK Payment of Wages Act, Section 9 — "Deductions may be made under clause (b) of sub-section (2) of section 7 only on account of the absence of an employed person from the place or places where, by the terms of his employment, he is required to work, such absence being for the whole or any part of the period during which he is so required to work"]. 
The amount of such deduction is also regulated, as stated in the same section, "The amount of such deduction shall in no case bear to the wages payable to the employed person in respect of the wage-periods for which the deduction is made a larger proportion than the period for which he was absent bears to the total period, within such wage-periods, during which by the terms of his employment, he was required to work" [KPK Payment of Wages Act, Section 9 — "The amount of such deduction shall in no case bear to the wages payable to the employed person in respect of the wage-periods for which the deduction is made a larger proportion than the period for which he was absent bears to the total period, within such wage-periods, during which by the terms of his employment, he was required to work"]. 
Note: This is general legal information, not legal advice. For your specific situation, consult a qualified lawyer or contact your provincial Labour Department.

query: Is there a law that gives workers 60 days of sick leave per year in Pakistan?
retrieved_laws: Punjab Shops and Establishments Ordinance S15
West Pakistan Shops and Establishments Ordinance S15
KPK Shops and Establishment Act S16
response:
There is no law that gives workers 60 days of sick leave per year in Pakistan. According to the Punjab Shops and Establishments Ordinance, Section 15, "Every employee shall be entitled to sick leave with full wages for a total period of eight days in every year" [Punjab Shops and Establishments Ordinance, Section 15 — "Every employee shall be entitled to sick leave with full wages for a total period of eight days in every year"]. Similarly, the West Pakistan Shops and Establishments Ordinance, Section 15, and the KPK Shops and Establishment Act, Section 16, also provide for eight days of sick leave with full wages in a year [West Pakistan Shops and Establishments Ordinance, Section 15 — "Every employee shall be entitled to sick leave with full wages for a total period of eight days in every year"], [KPK Shops and Establishment Act, Section 16 — "Every employee shall be entitled to sick leave with full wages for a total period of eight days in every year"]. The Factories Act, Section 49G, provides for sixteen days’ sick leave on half average pay in a year [Factories Act, Section 49G — "Every worker shall be entitled to sixteen days’ sick leave on half average pay in a year"], and the Mines Act, Section 28D, provides for sixteen days' sick leave on half average wages in a year [Mines Act, Section 28D — "Every person employed in a mine shall be entitled in a year to ten days' casual leave on full wages and sixteen days' sick leave on half average wages"]. 

Note: This is general legal information, not legal advice. For your specific situation, consult a qualified lawyer or contact your provincial Labour Department.
